question.text: Mr. Vilayati, a UK citizen, becomes a Resident and Ordinarily Resident (ROR) of India in FY 2026-27. He earns Rs.5,00,000 as interest on UK government bonds. The UK deducts tax at 10% under the India-UK DTAA. India's applicable tax rate is 30%. What is the correct tax treatment in India?

answer.text: The UK bond interest is exempt in India as it is already taxed in the UK
India will tax the full Rs.5,00,000 at 30% without any credit, resulting in Rs.1,50,000 tax
India will tax Rs.5,00,000 at 30% (Rs.1,50,000) and give credit for UK tax of Rs.50,000, resulting in Rs.1,00,000 net tax in India
India will tax only the difference between the two rates, i.e., 20% of Rs.5,00,000 = Rs.1,00,000, with no credit mechanism

explanation.text: **The Logic:** As an ROR in India, Mr. Vilayati is taxed on worldwide income. India taxes the UK bond interest at 30% = Rs.1,50,000. Under the Credit Method (which India follows), credit is given for tax paid in the UK: Rs.50,000 (10% of Rs.5,00,000). Net Indian tax = Rs.1,50,000 - Rs.50,000 = Rs.1,00,000. **The Trap:** While the net amount is Rs.1,00,000 in both options C and D, the mechanism matters. Option D incorrectly suggests India directly applies a differential rate — the correct mechanism is full taxation with credit. Option A wrongly applies the Exemption Method. **Cross-Exam Context:** This scenario mirrors the Mr. Vilayati example from the NISM curriculum, directly testable in Series X-B. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** The credit method means the effective tax rate is the higher of the two countries' rates — advise clients to factor this into expected post-tax returns on foreign investments.
